summary: Indian Key was the site of a terrifying attack on August 7th, 1840, when Indians assaulted the island, burning homes and killing many. My family and others barely escaped the massacre, hiding in a turtle crawl beneath our house before fleeing by boat. The island was ultimately reduced to dust, and many inhabitants like the Perrinos and Henry Goodyear survived by miraculous escapes.

Dr. Perrino was my father, who bravely tried to pacify the attacking Indians by speaking to them in Spanish and explaining he was a physician. Despite his efforts from the upper piazza, he was found and killed by the Indians while my mother and siblings hid below. His death was a tragic moment during the Indian Key massacre in 1840.

I am Henry Perrino, and during the Indian Key attack in 1840, I chose to escape rather than be burned alive in the cellar where my family hid. I forced aside a palmetto post to get out and helped my mother and sisters escape through a hole into the water. At just thirteen, I skillfully handled a launch boat to get us out of rifle range and eventually to safety aboard the schooner Medium.

I am Mrs. Perrino, who, along with my three children, hid beneath our house in the cold water during the Indian Key massacre in 1840. Despite the danger, I managed to dig through marl and create an opening so we could escape the burning cellar. Together with Henry and my daughters, we fled by boat to safety, enduring a terrifying night of violence and loss.

After the Indian Key massacre, the U.S.S. Flirt, commanded by Lieutenant McLaughlin, came to assist the survivors. We were rescued by the Flirt and then transferred to the government steamer Santee. The Flirt played a crucial role in our escape and safety along the Florida coast in 1840.

Lieutenant McLaughlin commanded the U.S.S. Flirt, which came to our aid after the Indian Key massacre in 1840. Under his leadership, we were rescued from danger and transported safely to St. Augustine aboard the steamer Santee. His prompt action was vital to our survival.

The government steamer Santee took us from the U.S.S. Flirt to St. Augustine after the Indian Key massacre in 1840. It provided safe passage along the Florida coast, allowing us to reach a place where we received aid and shelter. The Santee was an important part of our journey to safety.

After escaping the Indian Key massacre, we were taken to St. Augustine, Florida, where the citizens kindly supplied us with necessities and offered generous assistance. It was a place of refuge and relief following the horrors we endured. From there, with help from Dr. Edward Worrell, we secured passage to New York.

Dr. Edward Worrell, U.S.A., was very kind to my family after the Indian Key massacre. He helped us secure free passage by water and railroad from St. Augustine to Auburn, New York, so we could return to our former home. His generosity was a blessing during a very difficult time.

After the Indian Key massacre, my family and I traveled to Auburn, New York, thanks to the kindness of Dr. Edward Worrell who arranged free passage for us. From Auburn, we continued to Palmyra, our former home, by carriage. Auburn was a key stop on our journey back to safety in 1840.

Palmyra, New York, was our former home to which we returned after the Indian Key massacre in 1840. After arriving in Auburn, we took a half-fare carriage ride to Palmyra, seeking comfort and familiarity following the tragedy. It was a place of refuge and new beginnings for my family.

Henry Goodyear was one of the survivors of the Indian Key massacre, like my family. He was associated with the Goodyear Rubber Company and managed to escape the brutal attack that destroyed the island. His survival was part of the many miraculous escapes from that tragic event.

The Goodyear Tire and Rubber Company is connected to Henry Goodyear, who survived the Indian Key massacre in 1840. While the company itself is not detailed in the story, Henry's association with it is noted among those who escaped the tragedy. It represents part of the broader historical context of the people involved.

During the Indian Key massacre, the interior of the Cape Florida Light was burned while the keeper was on the summit of the high wall. The keeper was rescued in a remarkable way by flying a kite and using the line to lower himself down safely. This lighthouse incident was part of the broader chaos along the Florida coast at that time.

I sailed to California in 1849 aboard the Bustin G. Owens, a ship that rounded Cape Horn with 169 passengers. The passage fare was $250 for a cabin, and the voyage was marked by sightings of porpoises, Portuguese men-of-war, and other sea life. This journey was part of my decision to join the California gold rush after leaving New York.

Cape Horn was a significant milestone on my 1849 voyage to California aboard the Bustin G. Owens. We sailed around this treacherous southern tip of South America, enduring the full force of southeast trade winds. Passing Cape Horn was both a challenge and a symbol of our long journey to the west coast.

During the voyage to California, we saw beautiful Portuguese men-of-war in the tropics. These sea creatures had colorful red, purple, and blue hues and a sail-like structure on their backs that allowed them to be wafted over the waves. Their radiant colors reminded me of the vibrant scenes at Indian Key before the massacre.

On June 1st during our voyage, some passengers rowed over to the barque Simlar, which was bound for London, to send letters home. This brief interaction broke the monotony of the long sea journey aboard the Bustin G. Owens. It was a small but meaningful connection to the outside world.

On June 2nd, while sailing to California, we spoke with the Norwegian barque Antelope, which was coming from California and bound for Gottenburg. This encounter was one of several ship meetings that punctuated the monotony of our long voyage around Cape Horn. It was interesting to exchange news and greetings with fellow sailors.

During our voyage, we also met the French barque Sophie Cesar, which was sailing from Mozambique to Harve-deo-Grace. This encounter added variety to the journey and connected us with distant parts of the world. Such meetings were highlights amid the long days at sea.

entity: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner
summary: While at sea, I compared the vessel and the ocean to the scenes described in The Rime of the Ancient Mariner. On the Sabbath, I preferred solitude and reflection over attending a service led by a man who was less than pious. This literary comparison helped me make sense of the vast and sometimes eerie sea journey.
cues: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner

summary: On June 14th during our voyage to California, we crossed the Equator. This was a notable event as we entered the southern hemisphere and encountered new sea life, including a species of shark called the dog-fish. Crossing the Equator marked a significant point in our long journey around South America.

On June 16th, we passed the dreaded Cape St. Roque, feeling the full force of the southeast trade winds. This was a challenging part of our voyage to California aboard the Bustin G. Owens. We hoped to reach Rio de Janeiro about a week later, but the sea held us back.

On June 24th, instead of reaching Rio, we were becalmed about 30 miles from Cape Frio, where the lighthouse was visible. This delay was frustrating after weeks at sea, but it gave us a chance to observe the coast of Brazil and the local wildlife. Cape Frio was a landmark signaling our nearing destination.

While near Cape Frio, we saw a bird called the marlinspike, which resembled the frigate bird of Florida. This sighting was one of many natural wonders during the voyage to California. The marlinspike added to the rich experience of observing unfamiliar wildlife on the journey.

The frigatebird, which I had seen in Florida, was similar to the marlinspike bird spotted near Cape Frio during our voyage. These birds were a familiar sight that connected my past experiences with the new environments we encountered at sea. Their graceful flight was a comforting presence on the long journey.

On June 25th, as we neared the entrance to the harbor, we gazed upon the coast of Brazil. The scenery was grand and beautiful, with the famous Sugarloaf Mountain visible nearby. This marked an important moment as we approached Rio de Janeiro after weeks at sea around Cape Horn.

Sugarloaf Mountain, near Rio de Janeiro, was a striking landmark we saw as we entered the harbor on June 25th. The peak is about 900 feet high and resembles a loaf of sugar with its base partially melted. Its unique shape and grandeur made a lasting impression on me during our arrival in Brazil.

We anchored near Rio de Janeiro on June 25th after a long and eventful voyage. The city was about three miles inland, and we hired a boat to explore it. I remember the bustling harbor filled with boats rowed by negro slaves, tiled roofs, and lively markets with fruits, vegetables, and even monkeys. Rio was a vibrant and exotic place that contrasted sharply with the open sea.
